questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with a 2-week history of easy bruising, fatigue, and two episodes of epistaxis. She has no significant past medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, there is no lymphadenopathy or organomegaly. Blood tests show: Hb 85 g/L, MCV 94 fL, WBC 2.1 × 10^9/L, neutrophils 0.8 × 10^9/L, platelets 22 × 10^9/L. Bone marrow aspirate shows a markedly hypocellular marrow with no abnormal infiltrates. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The pancytopenia with a hypocellular marrow and absence of organomegaly or blasts is classic for aplastic anaemia. The most tempting distractor is myelodysplastic syndrome, which typically shows a normocellular or hypercellular marrow with dysplasia, not severe hypocellularity.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man is found to have pancytopenia on routine blood tests. He feels mildly fatigued but has no bleeding or infection. Examination is unremarkable. Hb 100 g/L, MCV 104 fL, WBC 3.2 × 10^9/L, neutrophils 1.5 × 10^9/L, platelets 95 × 10^9/L. B12 and folate levels are normal. Serum ferritin is elevated. Bone marrow biopsy shows moderate hypocellularity (30% cellularity) with no dysplasia or fibrosis. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: A hypocellular marrow without dysplasia or fibrosis points to aplastic anaemia. Hypocellular MDS is possible but uncommon and usually shows some dysplasia. Megaloblastic anaemia is excluded by normal B12/folate and the marrow appearance. No organomegaly makes hypersplenism unlikely.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man presents with fever, fatigue, and bruising 6 weeks after a self-limiting episode of acute hepatitis A (confirmed by serology). He is not on any medication. Examination reveals pallor but no lymphadenopathy or hepatosplenomegaly. Hb 78 g/L, WBC 1.5 × 10^9/L, neutrophils 0.3 × 10^9/L, platelets 15 × 10^9/L. Bone marrow aspirate is severely hypocellular. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Post-hepatitis aplastic anaemia is a well-recognised entity, typically occurring weeks to months after acute hepatitis. The severe pancytopenia with hypocellular marrow confirms it. Viral myelosuppression usually resolves quickly and is less severe; leukaemia would show blasts. PNH often has haemolysis.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman at 22 weeks gestation presents with progressive fatigue and petechiae. She has no prior obstetric complications. Examination shows no splenomegaly. Bloods: Hb 92 g/L, WBC 2.8 × 10^9/L, neutrophils 1.2 × 10^9/L, platelets 38 × 10^9/L. Bone marrow biopsy is hypocellular for gestation. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Pancytopenia with a hypocellular marrow in pregnancy is consistent with aplastic anaemia. Gestational thrombocytopenia causes isolated thrombocytopenia. HELLP and acute fatty liver feature liver dysfunction and hypertension. Megaloblastic anaemia lacks neutropenia and thrombocytopenia typically.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents with recent-onset fatigue and recurrent gum infections. He has no family history of note. Full blood count shows: Hb 109 g/L, MCV 96 fL, WBC 2.5 × 10^9/L, neutrophils 0.9 × 10^9/L, platelets 85 × 10^9/L. Lactate dehydrogenase is normal, and there is no haemoglobinuria. Bone marrow biopsy demonstrates severe hypocellularity (15% cellularity) without dysplasia or fibrosis. Flow cytometry for PNH is negative. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: Severe hypocellularity with no dysplasia, negative PNH flow, and no drug exposure strongly supports aplastic anaemia. Hypocellular MDS would show some dysplastic features. Drug-induced agranulocytosis typically spares red cells and platelets. ALL would show lymphoblasts. PNH excluded by flow.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with a 3-week history of bruising and fatigue. Full blood count shows Hb 80 g/L, WCC 2.1 x10^9/L, neutrophils 0.9 x10^9/L, platelets 15 x10^9/L. Blood film confirms pancytopenia with no blasts. What is the single most appropriate investigation to confirm the diagnosis?
options: Peripheral blood flow cytometry for PNH
Bone marrow aspiration and trephine biopsy
Serum vitamin B12 and folate levels
questions.explanation: Bone marrow examination is essential to confirm aplastic anaemia by demonstrating hypocellularity and excluding infiltrative disorders. The most tempting distractor is autoantibody screen (ANA, dsDNA) because lupus can cause cytopenias, but the clinical picture and blood film make aplastic anaemia more likely; bone marrow is definitive.

questions.stem: A 52-year-old man with recently diagnosed aplastic anaemia has the following peripheral blood results: Hb 95 g/L, MCV 102 fL, WCC 2.8 x10^9/L, neutrophils 1.2 x10^9/L, platelets 25 x10^9/L, reticulocyte percentage 0.5% (corrected reticulocyte count 0.3%). Bone marrow biopsy shows <25% cellularity. According to Camitta criteria, which of the following best classifies his disease severity?
questions.explanation: Camitta criteria for severe aplastic anaemia require marrow cellularity <25% and at least two of: neutrophils <0.5, platelets <20, reticulocytes <1% (or <20 x10^9/L corrected). Here neutrophils (1.2) and platelets (25) are above thresholds, and only reticulocytes meet criterion (<1%), so only one of three; thus non-severe. Very severe requires neutrophils <0.2.

questions.stem: A 68-year-old man presents with progressive fatigue and easy bruising. FBC shows Hb 85 g/L, WCC 2.5 x10^9/L, neutrophils 1.0 x10^9/L, platelets 18 x10^9/L. Blood film shows no blasts or dysplasia. Bone marrow aspirate is hypocellular with <5% blasts and no dysplastic features. A trephine biopsy shows moderate hypocellularity but no fibrosis. To confidently distinguish aplastic anaemia from hypoplastic myelodysplastic syndrome (MDS), which investigation is most appropriate?
options: Repeat bone marrow biopsy in 3 months
questions.explanation: Hypocellular MDS can mimic aplastic anaemia. Clonal chromosomal abnormalities (e.g., 5q-, 7q-) are characteristic of MDS and can be detected by cytogenetics, thus distinguishing it. Repeat biopsy is not the first step; cytogenetics is more definitive.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old woman with known severe aplastic anaemia is being considered for immunosuppressive therapy. She has no history of thrombosis or haemolytic episodes. As part of the pre-treatment workup, which investigation should be performed to screen for an associated condition?
options: Flow cytometry using FLAER for GPI-anchored proteins
questions.explanation: Paroxysmal nocturnal haemoglobinuria (PNH) is associated with aplastic anaemia. High-sensitivity flow cytometry with FLAER (detecting GPI-anchored proteins) is the recommended screening test. Ham and sucrose tests are less sensitive and outdated. Direct antiglobulin test screens for autoimmune haemolysis, not PNH.

questions.stem: A 9-year-old boy presents with pallor and petechiae. FBC shows pancytopenia. He has short stature, thumb hypoplasia, and café-au-lait spots. Bone marrow biopsy reveals hypocellular marrow with no blasts. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the suspected underlying diagnosis?
options: Chromosomal breakage study with diepoxybutane (DEB) on peripheral blood lymphocytes
Next-generation sequencing for Fanconi anaemia genes
questions.explanation: The clinical features suggest Fanconi anaemia (FA). The diagnostic gold standard is a chromosomal breakage test using DNA cross-linking agents like DEB; increased breakage confirms FA. NGS can identify mutations but is not first-line for confirmation. Telomere length is for dyskeratosis congenita.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old woman presents with fatigue and easy bruising. Hb 90 g/L, neutrophils 1.0×10⁹/L, platelets 30×10⁹/L. Reticulocyte count is 0.5%. Bone marrow aspirate and trephine show a hypocellular marrow with no dysplasia or fibrosis. She requires regular red cell transfusions. A matched sibling donor is not available. What is the most appropriate first-line treatment?
options: Matched unrelated donor stem cell transplant
questions.explanation: For transfusion-dependent acquired aplastic anaemia in an adult without a matched sibling donor, first-line is immunosuppressive therapy with ATG and ciclosporin. Eltrombopag is reserved for refractory disease. Oxymetholone and corticosteroids are not effective first-line treatments.

questions.stem: A 70-year-old man is found to have mild pancytopenia on routine bloods: Hb 120 g/L, neutrophils 1.5×10⁹/L, platelets 80×10⁹/L. He is asymptomatic. Bone marrow biopsy confirms hypocellularity consistent with aplastic anaemia. What is the most appropriate management?
options: Matched unrelated donor stem cell transplant
questions.explanation: In non-severe aplastic anaemia without transfusion dependence or symptoms, guidelines recommend observation and supportive care. Immunosuppression or transplant is reserved for progressive or severe disease.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man with severe aplastic anaemia (ANC 0.1×10⁹/L, platelets 5×10⁹/L, reticulocytes <1%) has a fully matched sibling donor. What is the definitive first-line treatment?
options: Matched sibling allogeneic stem cell transplant
questions.explanation: For severe aplastic anaemia in a patient younger than 40 with a matched sibling donor, allogeneic stem cell transplant is first-line due to its curative potential and superior long-term outcomes compared to immunosuppressive therapy.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with aplastic anaemia has received multiple red cell transfusions over 2 years. Her serum ferritin is 2500 ng/mL. She remains transfusion-dependent. Which therapy is indicated to manage a complication of her disease?
questions.explanation: Iron chelation is indicated for transfusion-induced iron overload. Deferasirox is the first-line oral chelator. Phlebotomy is contraindicated due to anaemia. Desferrioxamine is an alternative but not first-line.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old pregnant woman in the first trimester presents with severe aplastic anaemia (ANC 0.15×10⁹/L, platelets 8×10⁹/L). She requires transfusions. Which of the following therapies is contraindicated?
questions.explanation: Allogeneic stem cell transplant is contraindicated in pregnancy due to the teratogenic effects of conditioning chemotherapy and the risk to both mother and fetus. ATG and ciclosporin may be used with caution in severe cases. Eltrombopag and G-CSF have limited evidence but are not first-line.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents to the ED with a 3-week history of bruising, fatigue, and recurrent epistaxis. Full blood count: Hb 85 g/L, WBC 2.1 × 10^9/L, neutrophils 0.8 × 10^9/L, platelets 15 × 10^9/L. Blood film shows pancytopenia with no blasts or abnormal cells. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Urgent referral to haematology for bone marrow biopsy
Request CT chest, abdomen, and pelvis
Check serum vitamin B12 and folate
questions.explanation: The clinical scenario suggests aplastic anaemia. Urgent haematology referral for bone marrow biopsy is needed to confirm diagnosis and assess severity before initiating specific therapy. Corticosteroids are not first-line without confirmed immune aetiology; other investigations should not delay referral.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old man with known moderate aplastic anaemia (Hb 95 g/L, neutrophils 1.2 × 10^9/L, platelets 60 × 10^9/L) attends haematology clinic. He is asymptomatic, not transfusion-dependent, and has no significant bleeding. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Arrange HLA typing for possible transplant
questions.explanation: For non-severe aplastic anaemia in an asymptomatic patient, observation with regular FBC monitoring is appropriate according to BSH guidelines. Immunosuppression or transplant are reserved for severe or symptomatic cases; prophylactic antibiotics are not indicated without neutropenia.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old woman with known severe aplastic anaemia (neutrophils 0.3 × 10^9/L, platelets 10 × 10^9/L) presents with fever 39°C, rigors, and a central venous line in situ. She looks systemically unwell. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Transfuse one adult dose of platelets
questions.explanation: In a neutropenic septic patient with severe aplastic anaemia, the priority is to start empirical broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour after blood cultures. Antipyretics, platelet transfusion, or G-CSF are secondary; CT is not immediately warranted.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man with aplastic anaemia has received 80 units of red cells and 60 units of platelets over the past 2 years. His ferritin is 3200 μg/L, and he has normal cardiac function. He is currently stable with no active bleeding. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer for allogeneic stem cell transplant
questions.explanation: Iron overload from transfusions requires chelation therapy to prevent organ damage. Oral deferasirox is first-line in the UK. Venesection is not feasible in pancytopenia; vitamin C should not be given without chelation; transplant is for severe aplasia, not primarily for iron overload.

questions.stem: A 32-year-old primigravida at 28 weeks gestation with known aplastic anaemia is admitted with Hb 70 g/L (previously stable at 90) and platelets 30 × 10^9/L. She has no bleeding symptoms, and fetal movements are normal. What is the most appropriate next step?
options: Refer to haematology and obstetrics multidisciplinary team
Arrange elective caesarean section at 34 weeks
questions.explanation: Pregnancy with aplastic anaemia requires multidisciplinary input to balance maternal and fetal risks. Platelet transfusion is not indicated without bleeding or planned delivery. Immunosuppression and early delivery are decisions best made jointly, not as an immediate next step.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents with a 3-week history of fatigue and easy bruising. Full blood count shows Hb 90 g/L, WCC 2.0, neutrophils 0.6, platelets 20. Blood film shows no blasts or dysplasia. Which single finding is most indicative of severe aplastic anaemia?
questions.explanation: Severe aplastic anaemia is defined by bone marrow cellularity <25% (or <50% if cellularity 25-50%) plus at least two of three peripheral blood criteria: neutrophils <0.5, platelets <20, reticulocytes <20. While low reticulocytes are supportive, bone marrow cellularity is the gold standard for distinguishing from other causes of pancytopenia.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man with known severe aplastic anaemia on ciclosporin and antithymocyte globulin (ATG) presents with acute onset of headache, confusion, and hypertension (180/110 mmHg). He is afebrile. What is the most likely life-threatening complication?
questions.explanation: In patients with aplastic anaemia on ciclosporin, PRES is a well-recognised complication presenting with headache, confusion, seizures, and hypertension. Intracranial haemorrhage is less likely given no focal signs and absence of severe thrombocytopenia, while hypertensive encephalopathy is a less specific mimic. PRES is a 'can't-miss' emergency requiring prompt blood pressure control and discontinuation of ciclosporin.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman with aplastic anaemia in remission on eltrombopag develops dark urine and abdominal pain. Urine dipstick is positive for blood but microscopy shows no red cells. Which additional finding would confirm the suspicion of paroxysmal nocturnal haemoglobinuria (PNH)?
options: Flow cytometry showing CD55/CD59 deficiency on red cells
questions.explanation: PNH is a known complication of aplastic anaemia. While raised LDH, low haptoglobin, and high reticulocytes suggest haemolysis, the diagnostic gold standard is flow cytometry showing lack of GPI-anchored proteins (CD55/CD59) on red and white cells. Direct Coombs test is positive in autoimmune haemolytic anaemia, not PNH.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old man with newly diagnosed aplastic anaemia has a platelet count of 12 x10^9/L. He has no bleeding symptoms. Which finding is a 'can't-miss' red flag that indicates need for urgent intervention?
questions.explanation: While severe thrombocytopenia is concerning, the absence of bleeding symptoms does not preclude intracranial haemorrhage. A headache in this setting is a red flag for CNS bleeding and requires immediate imaging and platelet transfusion. Fever with rigors would also be an emergency but is more suggestive of sepsis in neutropenia; headache is the most specific for life-threatening bleed.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old woman with aplastic anaemia on ATG and ciclosporin develops a fever of 39°C and a new productive cough. Her blood results: neutrophils 0.1 x10^9/L, platelets 40 x10^9/L. Which investigation finding is most likely to identify a life-threatening complication?
options: Blood cultures positive for Staphylococcus aureus
Urinary antigen positive for Streptococcus pneumoniae
High-resolution CT chest showing nodular lesions
questions.explanation: In patients with profound neutropenia (ANC <0.1), invasive fungal infections, particularly aspergillosis, are a leading cause of death. While bacterial pneumonia is also possible, nodular lesions on CT are classic for angioinvasive aspergillosis, and bronchoalveolar lavage culture confirming Aspergillus is the gold standard. This finding identifies a life-threatening complication requiring urgent antifungal therapy.
